title: Chapter 0: How To Read This Book
content:
# Tutor System Architecture

This book explains how the Tutor app works in a practical order: what the user sees first, which tools power each part, where data goes, and what happens in the background after common actions.

The goal is not to document every line of code. The goal is to help a new contributor understand the system without needing to already know React, Dexie, SSE, OpenRouter, Graphify, or the document-ingestion stack.

Use this flow:

1. Read the product map.
2. Learn the main tools in plain language.
3. Follow the Study, Chat, Memory, Revision, Analytics, and Admin paths.
4. Read the background workflow chapter to connect the pieces.
5. Use the maintenance chapter before changing architecture-sensitive code.

Graphify is the machine-readable architecture graph for repository navigation. This book is the human-readable guide shown in the Library.

title: Chapter 1: Product Map
content:
# Product Map

Tutor is a learning workspace for reading documents, asking an AI tutor questions, saving what was learned, and revising that knowledge later.

The current top-level app route is controlled by `activeView` in `src/store/index.ts`. The user-facing navigation exposes:

- **Study**: upload and read documents, annotate pages, and chat with the tutor.
- **Analytics**: view learning progress and usage signals.
- **Revision**: open built-in books, generated learning books, notes, concepts, and flashcards.
- **Admin**: opened from the Library Admin card; it shows traces, server console logs, and debug runs.

The app uses a paper-style Library/Revision area to contrast with the darker Study workspace. Built-in books such as this architecture book and the App Design Language book live in code, while user learning books live in the browser database.

A simple mental model:

```text
Study captures activity -> Chat answers and uses tools -> Memory stores useful learning -> Revision and Analytics read that memory -> Admin explains and verifies the system.
```

title: Chapter 2: Tools In Plain English
content:
# Tools In Plain English

These are the important tools and what they do in this app.

| Tool | Simple meaning | Where it appears |
| --- | --- | --- |
| React | Builds the screen from reusable components. | `src/App.tsx`, views, components |
| Vite | Runs the local dev server and builds the app quickly. | `npm run dev`, `npm run build` |
| TypeScript | Adds types so code mistakes are caught earlier. | `.ts` and `.tsx` files |
| Tailwind CSS | Utility classes for spacing, color, layout, and responsive design. | Most JSX class names |
| GSAP | Drives route transitions, cards, buttons, panels, and premium app animation. | `gsap` in views and components |
| Zustand | Small global state store for active route, PDF state, settings, messages, and usage. | `src/store/index.ts` |
| Dexie | A friendly wrapper around IndexedDB. It saves concepts, flashcards, learning books, and trace logs locally. | `src/memory/longterm.memory.ts` |
| dexie-react-hooks | Lets React screens update automatically when Dexie data changes. | Revision, Analytics, Admin |
| Express | The Node server that receives browser API calls. | `server.ts` |
| SSE | Server-Sent Events. The server streams chat chunks to the browser over one long HTTP response. | `/api/chat` |
| OpenRouter/OpenAI SDK | Sends model requests to DeepSeek, Qwen, Gemini, Claude, and OpenAI-compatible models. | `server.ts` |
| Deepgram | Voice and speech provider for voice-agent/listen/speak features. | voice routes in `server.ts` |
| Serper | Web search provider when the user explicitly needs live web sources. | `web_search` flow in `/api/chat` and `/api/voice-web-search` |
| react-pdf | Renders PDF pages in the Study view. | `src/components/PdfViewer.tsx` |
| Python extraction script | Classifies uploaded documents and extracts text/images before the learning book update. | `scripts/classify_and_extract.py` via `/api/documents/ingest` |
| Graphify | Repository architecture graph for coding agents and maintainers. | `graphify-out/`, GitHub Actions |

In short: React draws the app, GSAP animates the experience, Zustand remembers live UI state, Dexie stores learning records locally, Express talks to model/search/voice providers, and Graphify helps maintainers navigate architecture safely.

title: Chapter 3: Frontend Flow
content:
# Frontend Flow

The frontend starts in `src/App.tsx`. It reads `activeView` from Zustand and decides which screen to render.

Current screens:

- `StudyView`: the main learning workspace.
- `AnalyticsView`: loaded lazily when needed.
- `RevisionView`: loaded lazily and used as the Library.
- `AdminView`: loaded lazily from the Admin card.

Lazy loading means heavier screens are not loaded until the user opens them. That keeps the first workspace lighter.

`Navigation` changes the active view. The Settings button stays available outside Admin. Admin hides normal navigation because it has its own sidebar for DeepSeek Trace and Server Console.

The global store also tracks PDF state, selected text, chat messages, API keys, voice settings, voice-agent events, language, active learning book, and usage analytics. This is why a selected PDF quote can move from `PdfViewer` into `ChatPanel`, and why Revision can open a specific generated book from another part of the app.

title: Chapter 4: Study And Document Ingestion
content:
# Study And Document Ingestion

Study is where the learner uploads a PDF or image and starts reading.

When a file is uploaded:

1. `StudyView` creates a local browser URL for PDFs so `PdfViewer` can render the pages.
2. `StudyView` sends the file to `/api/documents/ingest`.
3. The server runs `scripts/classify_and_extract.py` through Python.
4. The script classifies the document as text-based, scanned, image-like, or mixed.
5. If the document has scanned pages or images, the server can ask a vision model to extract readable text, tables, diagrams, and layout cues.
6. The server returns extracted content and document metadata.
7. The app asks `/api/learning-book-update` to turn the extracted material into useful library notes.

`StudyView` keeps uploaded PDFs in a compact chip rail above the reader. Each chip selects a document and exposes a small remove control, while the dashed plus chip opens the file picker. The reader itself stays underneath as the full-height work surface.

`PdfViewer` is the reading surface. It uses `react-pdf` to draw pages and supports page navigation, zoom, fit-width, highlights, underlines, strikethrough, sticky notes, selected text, and the Ask Tutor action.

Important idea: the PDF page shown on screen and the extracted text used for memory are related but not the same thing. The page renderer helps the user read. The ingestion pipeline gives the background learning system text it can summarize and store.

title: Chapter 5: Chat And Tutor Tools
content:
# Chat And Tutor Tools

`ChatPanel` is the live tutor interface. It prepares the user message, gathers context, calls `/api/chat`, and reads a streaming response.

Before a message is sent, the shared brain-context packet builder may gather:

- selected PDF text
- the current page screenshot if the question is about the visible page
- relevant local memory from `MemoryOrchestrator.getRelevantContext()`
- the active learning book summary and recent concepts
- an active-book PDF manifest plus balanced excerpts from multiple ready PDFs in the active book
- interaction timing state such as chat, voice, selected-text, and web-search mode
- user settings such as model, persona prompt, language, and API keys

Typed chat creates a browser request id before retrieval starts. That same id is attached to the brain-context packet memory row, the retrieval row, the `/api/chat` stream, model-run rows, tool-job rows, and Admin request timelines. Live voice uses the voice session id for the same local packet and audit path, prioritizes book/document context before long memory when the live prompt is compacted, and reports added, ready, excerpted, pending/failed, and omitted PDF counts in telemetry.

The server route `/api/chat` uses SSE. SSE means the server sends small events over time instead of waiting for the entire answer. The browser receives events such as:

- `status`: thinking, web search, tool execution, synthesizing, streaming
- `chunk`: visible text from the assistant
- `reasoning_summary`: short user-facing trace notes
- `web_search_started`, `web_result`, `web_sources_complete`: live source-search state
- `done`: final answer, usage numbers, graph updates, flashcards, evaluated answers, and sources

The tutor can use tools:

- `look_at_current_page`: analyze the current page image when the user asks about what is visible.
- `web_search`: search live web sources only when the user asks or the answer needs fresh facts.
- `update_graph`: create or update a learned concept.
- `generate_flashcards`: produce cards when the user asks for revision or active recall.
- `evaluate_answer`: score a quiz or active-recall answer and let the browser record BKT evidence only when a real concept id and explicit evaluation are present. If the id comes from the active learning book, the browser first promotes the matching stored learning-book concept into the BKT `concepts` table.

Live voice uses the `/api/voice-agent` websocket, grouped transcript cards, a dark audio-reactive stage, typed-turn injection, local voice-agent events, and client-side tools that Admin can inspect. It can call `look_at_current_page` for current-page, visible-diagram, screen, and reading-context questions through `/api/voice-current-page`. It can call `web_search` for explicit web or freshness questions through `/api/voice-web-search`, and it can call `evaluate_answer` for scored active-recall turns while selected-text, active-document, and active-book questions stay source-material-first.

Web-search sources write source-card artifact rows. Generated flashcards write local `ArtifactRecord` provenance with `not_checked` citation state, so Admin can audit the batch before broader generated-artifact verification exists. Evaluated answers write verified evidence and mastery-delta rows only after the local evidence contract accepts the concept id and score or correct/incorrect outcome; unresolved concept promotion stays `missing_concept` instead of fabricating mastery.

The key rule is source priority: questions about this page, this document, selected text, or the screen should use the local source material first. Web search is reserved for live external facts or explicit web-search requests.

title: Chapter 6: Memory, Dexie, And The Library
content:
# Memory, Dexie, And The Library

Tutor stores learning data in the browser using Dexie. Dexie is not a remote database. It is a developer-friendly way to use IndexedDB, the database built into modern browsers.

The database is called `NeuralNestBrain`. It contains tables for:

- `concepts`: older concept graph records.
- `misconceptions`: weak spots or wrong ideas the learner may hold.
- `sessions`: study session summaries.
- `interactions`: user/tutor exchanges with local embeddings.
- `flashcards`: active recall cards and review dates.
- `traceLogs`: plain explanations of background actions.
- `learningBooks`: generated books for a topic or session.
- `learningBookConcepts`: concepts attached to a generated book.
- `learningEntries`: individual learning notes from a conversation.
- `evidenceEvents` and `masteryDeltas`: source-linked evidence and BKT mastery movement.
- `memoryEvents`, `retrievalEvents`, `modelRuns`, `toolJobs`, and `backgroundJobs`: local observability ledgers.
- `correctionEvents`, `artifactRecords`, and `citationStates`: local trust, source-grounding, generated-artifact provenance, and correction-control records.

`MemoryOrchestrator` is the bridge between chat, voice, and the library. Chat and voice turns pass request metadata into the memory layer so Admin can group the foreground request with the background interaction, learning-book, and graph rows. Interaction-memory capture, learning-book updates, and graph-concept updates also write durable local background job state with queued, running, completed, retry-scheduled, and dead-letter statuses. After a tutor answer finishes, it:

1. stores the interaction locally,
2. asks `/api/learning-book-update` for structured notes,
3. writes or updates a learning book,
4. writes concepts, entries, and generated learning-note artifact provenance,
5. attaches compact source-span preview anchors when document text exists,
6. runs the initial local provenance check for the generated note,
7. announces the active book with a browser event,
8. optionally logs a trace explanation through `/api/trace`.

Generated flashcard artifacts can be locally checked in Admin for coherent batch, card id, message, local-only, and no-external-fetch provenance. That proves the cards are traceable to saved local rows; it does not prove the card answers are factually correct. Generated learning-note artifacts run the same local provenance style automatically when Memory writes them, and Admin can re-run the check for coherent entry, book or conversation, local-only, no-external-fetch metadata, and saved source-span anchors when document text was available. Notes with spans now also report whether each compact saved summary claim has local lexical support in a source preview. That proves inspectable local traceability and preview-level term overlap; it does not prove semantic entailment, factual truth, or document-wide grounding. Built-in chapter audio guides also seed `audio_overview` artifact rows when Admin loads. Admin can now locally check their manifest integrity: local MP3 path, overview id, book/chapter anchors, transcript length, summary, voice, measured `durationSeconds` inside the 180-245 second local beta window, stored date, and no-external-fetch provenance. That keeps generated local assets in the same review ledger as runtime artifacts without claiming audio transcription or factual source-span verification.

Model summaries can propose concept mastery and confidence, but durable learner mastery and learner confidence stay gated. The background memory agent stores accepted values, the model_observation_v1 contract, non-verified status, and no-mutation gates in evidence and memory metadata instead of raising those learner-state fields from summaries alone. Validated flashcard reviews and evaluated learner answers linked to real concept ids can move BKT mastery and durable learner confidence with capped recall-evidence deltas, and those before/after confidence values, rubric/score fields, request anchors, and concept-promotion status are written in evidence metadata. Active learning-book concept ids are promoted into persistent BKT concepts only when the matching stored book concept exists.

Correction controls are non-destructive in local beta. When Admin marks a row wrong, requests deletion, or supersedes a record, affected ledgers receive correction metadata and conservative stale/skipped states instead of being hard-deleted. Concept-level corrections also quarantine learner-state scores locally: confidence is cleared, mastery and BKT knowledge are capped, and the previous values stay in `correctionState` for review.

This is why the Library can keep growing even when chat messages are cleared. Chat is the live conversation. Dexie is the learner notebook.

title: Chapter 7: Revision And Active Recall
content:
# Revision And Active Recall

`RevisionView` is both the Library and the review surface.

It shows two kinds of books:

- **Built-in books**: code-defined books such as Tutor System Architecture, User Brain Architecture, and App Design Language.
- **Generated learning books**: books saved in Dexie after document uploads and tutor conversations.

Every built-in chapter attaches a stored 3-4 minute audio guide. These guides are Deepgram-generated MP3 assets played by one visible browser player with pause, speed, and seek controls, not live read-aloud calls. A hidden audio element keeps bounded retry playback inside that same player so the learner does not see fallback controls or a second play button. The audio manifest stores measured `durationSeconds`, the dry run prints those seconds, and the local test suite can compare checked-in MP3 durations against the manifest when `ffprobe` or `afinfo` is available. The guides can be regenerated through the Deepgram provider when `DEEPGRAM_API_KEY` is available.

Generated books use the `learningBooks`, `learningBookConcepts`, `learningEntries`, and `flashcards` tables. Revision can show chapters, concept summaries, due flashcards, and review cards. A flashcard review updates `nextReviewAt`, which is a simple spaced-repetition schedule. When that card is linked to a real concept, the review also writes validated generation evidence, moves BKT mastery, and nudges durable learner confidence up or down through capped evidence deltas. The same evidence boundary now exists for evaluated learner answers: typed chat and live voice can move BKT only after `evaluate_answer` supplies a real concept id plus score or explicit correct/incorrect outcome, and active-book concept ids are promoted into persistent BKT concepts only when that stored learning-book concept is present.

The practical purpose of Revision is simple: move learning out of the chat transcript and into reusable study material. A learner should be able to come back later and understand the topic without replaying the entire conversation.

title: Chapter 8: Analytics And Admin
content:
# Analytics And Admin

Analytics and Admin are reader surfaces for system state.

**Analytics** reads local learning records and usage counters. It summarizes progress, mastery, confidence, interactions, and study activity. It does not own the learning flow; it visualizes what Study, Chat, and Memory already captured.

**Admin** is for diagnostics and local beta control. It contains:

- **System Activity**: local backend events plus request timelines that group brain-context packet injections, retrieval injections, model runs, tool jobs, interaction/learning-book/graph-concept background job state, and background memory writes by request id.
- **Model Runs**: durable records for model starts, fallbacks, completions, blocked requests, and failures.
- **Voice Agent Timeline**: local websocket, Deepgram settings, speaking/listening, barge-in, transcript-turn, current-page vision, web-search, evaluated-answer, and error events from live voice mode.
- **Memory Events** and **Retrieval Events**: local rows for learner-brain writes, shared chat/voice context packets, and context selection, including request ids when a chat or voice turn injected that context.
- **Correction Requests**: mark wrong, deletion review, supersede, dismiss, block, and non-destructive propagation overlays.
- **Source Artifacts** and **Citation States**: source cards, generated flashcard, learning-note, and chapter audio-guide artifact rows, and citation-state rows captured from chat, memory, tool results, and built-in manifests, plus local verifiers that check saved source-card links, generated flashcard provenance, generated learning-note provenance, and stored audio-guide manifest integrity without fetching external pages.
- **Evidence Ledger**: verified evidence events and BKT mastery deltas from flashcard reviews and evaluated learner answers.
- **Runtime Tuning**: local settings for source-vs-web behavior, tool loop budget, memory context, and refresh cadence.
- **Beta Diagnostics**: capped local JSON export plus brain-flow coverage for chat context injection, voice context injection, chat and voice multi-PDF context evidence, request correlation, chat and voice foreground tool jobs, chat and voice request-correlated evaluated mastery evidence, chat and voice transcript persistence, request-correlated chat and voice learner-memory writes, model-observation gates, and background job dead-letter blocking before broader beta claims. Each signal can show compact live request ids, row sources, latest timestamps, and context PDF ids when proof exists. It also offers a clearly separated deterministic synthetic wiring rehearsal through the shared multi-PDF packet helpers, typed-chat and live-voice tool definitions, matching shared tool schemas, and the same thirteen-signal verifier. Rehearsal rows stay in memory only and cannot count toward live readiness.
- **DeepSeek Trace Ledger**: saved trace explanations and learning-book evidence. It explains recorded model actions, not hidden chain-of-thought.
- **Server Console**: live backend messages over WebSocket.

Admin helps answer questions such as:

- What happened during one tutor request?
- Which local brain-context packet was injected before the model or voice agent answered?
- Did chat and voice both receive multiple ready PDFs from the active learning book?
- Which request id, row source, timestamp, or context PDF id proves a beta signal?
- Do chat and voice still expose the same required fields for shared tools?
- Did a background learning-book update happen?
- Is a background job queued, retrying, completed, or dead-lettered?
- Which model, tool, retrieval, memory, voice, evidence, artifact, or correction row was recorded?
- Did a flashcard review or evaluated answer move BKT, and what rubric or score metadata explains it?
- Is a remembered learner fact source-linked, reviewable, and reversible?
- Is a citation row locally verified, still checking, unavailable, unsupported, not checked, or conflicting?
- Is the backend receiving requests and streaming logs?

The important boundary: Admin reports recorded artifacts. It should not pretend to know private model internals beyond the traces, summaries, events, and files the app actually saved.

title: Chapter 9: A Background Workflow Example
content:
# A Background Workflow Example

This chapter follows a simple process: the learner uploads a PDF and asks the tutor to explain the current page.

## Step 1: Upload

The learner drops a PDF into Study. `StudyView` saves a temporary browser URL for reading and sends the file to `/api/documents/ingest`.

## Step 2: Extract

The server asks the Python extraction script to classify and extract the document. If needed, page images are sent to a vision model so scanned text or diagrams can become markdown-like source context.

## Step 3: Render

`PdfViewer` renders the PDF page in the browser. The user can highlight text, add notes, move pages, or press Ask Tutor.

## Step 4: Ask

`ChatPanel` builds the message. If the user asks about the current page, it captures a compressed image of the visible PDF canvas. It also asks the shared brain-context packet builder for relevant memory, active-book summary, an active-book PDF manifest, balanced excerpts from multiple ready PDFs, and interaction timing state from Dexie. Pending, failed, ready, excerpted, and omitted PDFs are kept in packet metadata so the request does not silently collapse to only the PDF on screen.

## Step 5: Stream

The browser calls `/api/chat` with the packet text and request id. The server sends SSE events. The user sees the answer appear gradually, while status and reasoning-summary events explain high-level progress such as retrieving context, looking at the page, or reviewing web sources.

## Step 6: Use Tools

If the model asks to inspect the current page, the server calls the page-vision tool. Voice mode can now make the same kind of page-inspection request through `/api/voice-current-page` and return the text result into the voice tool loop. If the user asked for flashcards, the flashcard tool is used. If the learner answers a quiz or active-recall prompt and a real local concept id is available, the evaluate-answer tool can stage explicit evidence. Active-book concept ids are resolved from stored learning-book concepts before BKT is allowed to move. If fresh external facts are needed, web search can run through Serper.

## Step 7: Finish

The final `done` event includes the complete answer, usage numbers, sources, graph updates, possible flashcards, and possible evaluated-answer payloads. Web sources persist as source-card artifacts; generated flashcard batches persist as not-checked artifact provenance that Admin can locally verify for saved card ids, batch/message anchors, local-only metadata, and no external fetch. Evaluated-answer payloads are recorded by the browser only when the local evidence contract accepts the concept id and evaluation, with unresolved concept promotion reported as `missing_concept` instead of invented.

## Step 8: Save Learning

After the answer, `MemoryOrchestrator` records durable background job state for interaction-memory capture, learning-book updates, and graph-concept updates. It stores the interaction, calls `/api/learning-book-update`, merges the returned chapter/concepts into Dexie, writes a learning entry, records that generated note as artifact provenance, attaches compact source-span preview anchors when document text exists, and immediately runs the local provenance plus preview-level lexical-support check. Evaluated answers that pass the evidence contract write verified evidence and mastery-delta rows beside the request timeline. The earlier `brain_context_injected` memory row remains on the same Admin request timeline as retrieval, model, tool, and background-job rows, so the user can see what local context was used before the answer and whether background memory work completed or dead-lettered. Admin can re-run generated-note checks to verify that the note still points to the expected learning entry, local book or conversation, saved source-span anchors, and local summary-preview to source-preview lexical support without fetching external pages. Shared terms are inspectable support, not fact proof.

## Step 9: Review Later

Revision reads the saved learning book and flashcards from Dexie. Analytics reads the same local records for progress views. Admin can show trace/debug evidence and artifact provenance for background activity.

That is the core loop: upload, extract, read, ask, stream, store, revise.

title: Chapter 10: Model And Provider Map
content:
# Model And Provider Map

Most cloud model calls go through `server.ts`. The browser sends the user's OpenRouter key as a bearer token when available, so each learner can bring their own key. A server OpenRouter key is not the default product key; it is used only when the deployment owner explicitly sets `ALLOW_SERVER_OPENROUTER_FALLBACK=true`.

| Job | Main provider/model | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tutor chat | OpenRouter, `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` by default | Stream the main answer. |
| Chat fallback models | Gemini, Claude Haiku, GPT-4o mini, Llama Maverick | Keep chat working if the chosen model fails. |
| Learning-book update | OpenRouter, `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Convert a conversation into book chapters, concepts, risks, and notes. |
| Trace explanation | OpenRouter, `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Explain a saved background action in plain language. |
| Flashcards | OpenRouter, `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | Generate active recall cards. |
| Page/document vision | Qwen vision or GPT-4o mini depending on route | Read page images, scanned pages, diagrams, and visible content. |
| Persona generation | Claude Sonnet through OpenRouter | Turn a persona description into a professional system prompt. |
| Web search | Serper | Fetch current web/news sources when needed. |
| Live voice and read-aloud | Deepgram plus OpenAI speech fallback | Listen/speak voice features and optional live read-aloud audio. |
| Stored chapter guides | Deepgram `aura-2-odysseus-en` at speed `1`, using GPT-authored 3-4 minute scripts, measured `durationSeconds`, and checked-in MP3 assets | Explain built-in chapters without live TTS at playback time. |
| Pricing | OpenRouter pricing endpoint | Estimate usage cost. |

The app should always distinguish local source-material questions from current external-fact questions. Local document questions should use the uploaded material first. Current news, pricing, laws, release dates, and similar topics may need live web sources.

title: Chapter 11: Graphify, Debugging, And Safety
content:
# Graphify, Debugging, And Safety

Graphify is the repository architecture graph for coding agents. It replaces the old custom architecture runtime and stores generated graph artifacts under `graphify-out/`.

Use Graphify before broad repository reads:

```bash
graphify query "task description" --budget 2000 --graph graphify-out/graph.json
graphify path "SourceSymbol" "TargetSymbol" --graph graphify-out/graph.json
npm run graphify:tree
```

Rebuild policy:

- Do not rebuild the architecture graph after every chat turn, save, local change, commit, or checkout.
- There is no GitHub Actions workflow that refreshes Graphify on push or pull request.
- Regenerate `graphify-out` only for explicit local maintenance, agent-requested graph work, or a task that includes Graphify artifact updates.
- Pull requests should run lint/build quality gates without rewriting graph artifacts unless Graphify maintenance is in scope.

Do not manually edit generated Graphify artifacts. Let an explicit `graphify update . --force` plus `npm run graphify:tree` refresh them.

title: Chapter 12: Maintenance Boundaries
content:
# Maintenance Boundaries

Some files are safe to edit as content. Others are architecture-sensitive.

Lower-risk content edits:

- `src/lib/tutorBook.json` for this built-in book.
- UI copy that does not change API or data shape.
- Styling changes inside one view, after visual verification.

Higher-risk areas:

- Dexie schema in `src/memory/longterm.memory.ts`.
- Server routes, SSE event shapes, WebSocket paths, and API contracts in `server.ts`.
- Zustand state fields in `src/store/index.ts`.
- Chat streaming parser and tool handling in `ChatPanel`.
- Document ingestion and Python extraction behavior.
- Generated Graphify artifacts in `graphify-out/`.

A good finish checklist for architecture/content work is:

```bash
npm run format:check
npm run lint
npm run build
```

If the change affects visual layout, also verify the relevant screen in the browser. If the change affects chat, API, or memory behavior, test the real workflow rather than only checking that TypeScript compiles.
